title: Chapter 7 – Recognition (3)
content:
"Do you have any questions about the nature of the evaluation?"

"No."

"You must be curious about the method. It's simple."

The examiner spoke in a flat tone.

"Perform the fastest sword draw you can. A single strike will suffice. I will observe and judge."

'That's simple.'

Jeong Yeon-shin felt relieved.

He had expected some complicated procedure.

Heon Won-chang looked anxious, but judging a person's skill from a single sword strike seemed both narrow-minded and generous at once.

In Jeong Yeon-shin's experience, real combat was full of variables.

The first test appeared to be nothing more than a screening.

"Shall I begin now?"

He asked, stepping back a few paces. The examiner nodded.

"I — I'll go first!"

It was Heon Won-chang. He shot Jeong Yeon-shin an apologetic look and mouthed:

'I'm too nervous. Sorry.'

Jeong Yeon-shin, who didn't mind in the slightest, nodded.

Heon Won-chang took the spot where the boy had been standing and gripped his sword.

He glanced at the examiner, who stood with arms folded as if to say draw whenever you please, then set his jaw and assumed his stance.

It wasn't an exceptionally polished stance, but Jeong Yeon-shin detected waves of energy rising from his entire body.

'He's strong.'

It was well-honed energy. Stronger than any of the wandering swordsmen he'd encountered on the road to Xiangyang.

'When energy is refined, there's no waste. You can channel everything into the sword.'

As Jeong Yeon-shin was considering this, Heon Won-chang drew his sword, and the air split.

"Impressive! That martial artist is different."

"I couldn't see it."

"I only saw a white blur."

The spectators exclaimed in admiration. Gamblers were already scrambling to place bets on whether he'd pass.

"Not bad."

The examiner nodded.

A bright smile bloomed across Heon Won-chang's face, and the examiner took his number tag.

Heon Won-chang returned to Jeong Yeon-shin's side and whispered.

"For this first test, if the examiner takes your tag, you pass. If he tells you to return it to the reception desk, you fail."

"How many times have you passed the first test?"

"Hmm. The second test — the martial arts match — was always the problem."

"Is there a third test?"

"An interview with the Lord of Desolate Sect. Some martial artists come to Xiangyang just for that. To gain insight and enlightenment."

"I see."

Jeong Yeon-shin nodded and stepped forward.

Those who had reacted differently to the outcome of bets on Heon Won-chang, and even those who had watched with skepticism, now showed puzzled expressions.

"Was he really an applicant?"

"A young swordsman with some spirit."

"Well, if he's from out of town, when else would he get a chance to demonstrate his swordwork before a Desolate Sect martial artist?"

"Even a single word of advice would be a valuable experience."

Among the murmuring crowd, the gamblers didn't even bother placing bets.

Some wore smiles as if watching a child at play.

Jeong Yeon-shin placed his hand on his sword in silence and looked at the examiner.

The examiner nodded, his expression unchanged.

"You may begin."

The next moment.

"……"

Nothing happened.

The only difference was that Jeong Yeon-shin's sword was now drawn.

It was as if the scene had been cut between blinks. Only the examiner nodded, his face rigid.

"The future of the Desolate Sect is here."

An extraordinary compliment. The spectators and Heon Won-chang reacted as though they couldn't believe what they'd heard.

Some gasped or turned to the person beside them, wondering if they'd misheard. Heon Won-chang's slack-jawed expression was quite the sight.

Jeong Yeon-shin was surprised for a different reason.

'Even I, who swung the sword, couldn't see it.'

It seemed the examiner had followed the lightning-fast strike that was invisible even to the swordsman's own eyes.

Was this what true eye technique meant? They said reading an opponent's attack was itself a martial art.

"Give me your tag."

"…Yes."

"Hmm. Number thirteen."

The examiner received the tag and traced the characters as if engraving them into memory.

His gaze, studying Jeong Yeon-shin intently, seemed to hold a tangle of emotions.

After a moment, as if resigned to something, he closed his eyes once and nodded. It meant to go.

"Then."

Jeong Yeon-shin sheathed his sword, clasped his fists in salute, and left the training ground.

Heon Won-chang, who had been standing frozen, hurried after him.

The glances from nearby were mildly irritating.

"What is it?"

"Um, are you… perhaps the son of a famous clan?"

Jeong Yeon-shin laughed at the careful deference.

"What difference would that make? I thought once you enter Desolate Sect, only martial skill, talent, and effort matter."

"Oh… you truly intend to pass the Desolate test."

"Isn't that why we came? Not just me, but you too, Brother Heon Won."

"…That's right. You're correct."

Heon Won-chang fell silent.

He was staying at a different inn, and after a stretch of quiet, he said his goodbyes and disappeared.

Jeong Yeon-shin hoped they would pass the second test together — that kind and talkative man.

* * *

The intervals between the first and second tests were one week and two months, respectively.

By the time Jeong Yeon-shin passed the first test, roughly three weeks remained until the second.

During that time, he practiced the Jeong Family Dynamic Technique, the Swift Sword Technique, and the energy manipulation method he had named Fate Conquering Scripture.

He trained all three in the mountains outside the town where the inn stood, and devoted himself to the Fate Conquering Scripture when he returned to his room.

While creating new martial arts was worthwhile, he believed it was right to hone his existing skills before facing real combat.

Martial arts match. Pitting the martial arts one has learned against an opponent.

The final Iphwang test was said to conclude by demonstrating one's martial prowess alongside the opponent.

Jeong Yeon-shin devoted every remaining moment solely to martial arts, and during that time, Heon Won-chang never visited.

-Murmuring.

And now.

"This is…"

Inside Desolate Sect, there were dozens of magnificent and splendid halls.

Every martial artist who passed by exuded a stern aura.

A cluster of grand halls nestled against spectacular natural scenery, with a horizon visible on the far side of the world.

While following an attendant's lead, Jeong Yeon-shin noticed others in the same situation.

"This is truly the world's finest place to study martial arts…"

"I've heard white uniforms mark junior martial artists, but how can their presence be so powerful?"

"Isn't it obvious? Even wearing white here guarantees a promising future."

"I wonder what kind of hidden dragon will emerge this time…"

"I, Cho Mulyang of Hangsan, will be the dragon that spreads its wings!"

They seemed to be strangers but were chatting easily, and then Jeong Yeon-shin spotted a familiar face.

Heon Won-chang had noticed him too, and approached with a delighted expression.

"Young Master Jeong! You seem to have grown taller!"

"Brother Heon Won, you look well."

"I've made some small gains."

Heon Won-chang, still wearing the conspicuous yellow dragon hero's headband, stroked his upper lip.

"But still, you never know. In this test, winning isn't what matters. They say it's enough to impress the Desolate Sect masters watching the matches, even if you only fight once out of two rounds… That's the hardest part."

"You mean you can fail even after winning a match."

"Exactly. What's more, today the examiners' standards are bound to be even higher."

"Why is that?"

"Haven't you heard the rumors? They say the White Qilin will appear at today's Desolate test."

"The White Qilin — from the Namgung clan?"

"That's right. I've heard he's already famous across South Zhili. Before turning fifteen, he annihilated a notorious bandit group and killed the Slaughtering Killer, a sword master of the dark path… Around eighteen, they say he defeated every rising disciple at the Dragon Phoenix Gathering of the Eight Great Clans. The Dragon Phoenix Gathering is where young disciples from famous clans socialize, and since no one denied the rumors, it might well be true, don't you think?"

This time, even Jeong Yeon-shin couldn't hide his surprise.

Such strength truly deserved the title of Qilin — a creature of legend.

The reality of the murim, which he had only caught fragments of in Xinye County, now felt so near that he truly sensed he had entered the martial world.

"Isn't being a disciple of the Namgung clan already enough? If he has martial prowess too, why come to Desolate Sect?"

"Because he's an illegitimate son. He can never become the clan leader."

Heon Won-chang leaned in and whispered behind his hand.

Jeong Yeon-shin flinched at the breath on his ear and stepped away.

Heon Won-chang chuckled, as if any trace of distance or deference had never existed.

"They say the Lord of Desolate Sect, being of noble lineage, has kept her youthful appearance for two hundred years, but even rising to the rank of group leader here would be no less enviable than leading one of the Eight Great Clans. The prestige might even be greater. After all, it's a position that coordinates the order of the murim from the very center of the Central Plains."

"Was it truly to that extent?"

"Isn't it only natural, given Desolate Sect's involvement in every major event in the murim? Are you from some village rather than a clan?"

Jeong Yeon-shin kept his mouth shut and walked on.

Heon Won-chang followed with a grin, looking at him as if he were a charming younger brother.

"Please wait here. When your name is called, proceed to the training ground."

The attendant left about thirty aspiring entrants behind and departed.

The place where they halted was a small garden behind a massive training ground.

There was another one on the opposite side, where people had also gathered.

Green sprouts pushing upward alongside scattered bursts of colorful petals. Yet no one spared the scenery a glance.

Each had their own posture — some standing with eyes closed in mental training, others staring blankly into empty space.

Jeong Yeon-shin waited while circulating his Jeong Family Dynamic Technique through subtle movements.

Eventually, one person from each side began to be called forth.

They engaged in martial arts matches at the center of a circle formed by seated Desolate Sect martial artists.

After each fight, those heading north walked with shoulders high enough to pierce the sky, while those returning south were visibly deflated.

"How can they be like that…"

Heon Won-chang again failed to mask his anxiety.

"Among the martial artists who passed the first test's screening, there's probably no one who hasn't been called a prodigy. I was called a martial arts genius back in my county."

His attempt to ease the tension with self-deprecating humor was almost pitiful.

"How cruel and difficult it is to be selected from among such prodigies. And to have to demonstrate martial arts under the gaze of Desolate Sect masters, no less."

"Isn't it exciting?"

"Huh?"

"Being recognized for your talent is thrilling. At the very least, it's twice as good as being dismissed as a weirdo."

"Young Master…"

Just as Heon Won-chang was about to say something —

"Namyang of Henan Province! Jeong Yeon-shin of Xinye County, come forward!"

An attendant who had run to the garden called out.

"I hope to see you again. Here, in Desolate Sect."

Jeong Yeon-shin, left hand resting on the sword at his hip, walked out of the garden with unhurried steps. Heon Won-chang's shout rang out behind him.

"Let's make sure we do!"

* * *

The first martial arts match was trivial.

The opponent was a peculiar fellow who spoke oddly, as though he had trained in isolation in some remote village.

They say there are the Four Books and Five Classics in the world, and various sects each carry their own teachings, but most martial artists who had never properly studied orthodox martial arts lived in worlds of their own making.

The only universal principle was survival of the fittest.

There were all manner of people in the Central Plains, making it impossible to predict another's values or actions.

"So you're the moth fluttering madly, blind to the despair that awaits!"

The swordsman grinned, baring yellow teeth.

The moment the match began, Jeong Yeon-shin struck the man's mouth with his scabbard.
